text: Hello and welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. Today we're talking about a
blockchain-powered solution from PricewaterhouseCoopers and I'm joined by Jen from PwC. Jen,
welcome to the show. Thank you for having me. Can you tell us a little bit more about what
your emerging tech team does? Yeah, so our emerging technology team at PwC is focused on
bringing new and emerging technologies to the work that we do on the daily basis. So we partner
with our sector experts, folks that are embedded in industries and specific clients, as well as with
our clients to co-create solutions to real-world problems. And some of those technologies that
your team works with is AI as well as blockchain. Exactly. So over the past few years we've actually
been focused on what we call our essential eight technologies and the convergence between those.
And blockchain, IoT, AI are just three of those technologies. Okay, great. So we're talking
material traceability, we're talking supply chain. Let's jump right in here and talk about the feedback
that you've received from your customers and your clients and what they're asking for. Let's do it.
So the way that we like to start off any possible solution is by starting with some design thinking.
So we hosted a workshop with about nine utility companies and asked them about the pain that they
feel in their daily processes. And the things that we heard over and over again was that everyone
involved in their supply chain process all have their own version of the truth. There's limited
end-to-end transparency within the entire supply chain. The other thing that we kept hearing is that
there's fairly stringent regulatory standards that they need to comply with. And in order to do that,
they need to have one reconciled version of the truth. And sometimes that gets a little bit manual
and a little bit costly. So we have transparency, we have compliance, we also have a number of different
users that are interacting with this entire end-to-end process. Can you talk to us a little bit about who
those users are? So when we're dealing with any kind of utility supply chain process, the second that
someone needs a material down at an oil rig or down at a job site, it can take a little bit of a while. We start with a project manager in SAP,
actually ordering that new supply, that new pipe, and then that will eventually go down to a supplier,
sometimes multiple tiers of suppliers. That would then get fed into a distributor. That might then get fed
into a series of other parties before finally making their way to that utility company. So what we wanted
to actually prove out with our blockchain solution is three different things along this process. So the first we
wanted to prove is this end-to-end transparency across this entire value chain.
The second thing that we really wanted to try to prove is this notion of actually maintaining our privacy and data standards, right?
The first question we always get asked when we talk about blockchain is, well, can't this get done in essential portal?
Well, not quite because then one party has to own it. Sure. Do we trust them? Then there is a single point of failure.
Do we trust that? And then one party might be able to see more information than they're allowed.
So we really wanted to prove that on the blockchain, we can actually maintain privacy between these parties.
So privacy being the second main thing that you guys really wanted to focus on?
Exactly. And the third thing is, look, we know that it's a complex process to introduce something new to an entire industry.
We wanted to demonstrate that it really doesn't have to be this hard. We wanted to demonstrate that this can integrate with existing workflows and existing systems.

So we wanted to actually show that this can integrate directly with SAP, the tool that our utility companies already know, and showcase that this really will integrate with the process they already follow, which actually kicks off with the purchase order.
So you guys have really allowed, from an integration perspective, an API call into an enterprise resource planning system that customers already own.
Exactly. Simple API call into SAP and then our information comes up onto the blockchain. Shared single source of truth.
Okay. So let's talk a little bit more specifics technically from what we're doing. Are we deploying hyperledger fabric and creating the actual ledger itself to interact with this entire process?
Exactly. So the way that we typically run POCs with our clients is we actually host them within our own environment.
And then of course, when we move to pilot, when we move to production, we think there's some additional considerations, hosting infrastructure integration,
before we select a cloud platform or a provider. But when we host on PwC, we're building on AWS and we're building on the Amazon managed blockchain,
which actually comes pre deployed with hyperledger fabric 1.2.
So let's talk a little bit about tracking assets now that we already have the integration from the ledger into the ERP system.
A lot of customers, they really rely on the importance of where their assets at, at any given time.
So tracking the location amongst other different types of telemetry. Can you talk to us about how you guys made a decision to leverage AWS IoT Core for this telemetry data?
We need to get our physical world onto our virtual world, our blockchain through IoT.
So the decision to use IoT made natural sense. When dealing with our really complex supply chain,
we wanted to make sure that we were capturing all of the things that happen in our physical world onto the blockchain.
We wanted to make sure we're capturing the location of different assets. We want to make sure we're capturing these really complex handoffs between different parties in the value chain.
So when it came to IoT, we actually spent a little bit more time in non-technical requirements than we did with technical requirements.
We spent a lot more time actually figuring out what we really needed to track in order to have meaningful data on the blockchain,
and who specifically needed to have access to get information onto the blockchain.
So we ended up settling on was different thresholds of location and movement for information to be added onto the chain,
as well as sensors that would come on at specific points throughout the day in order to be able to capture information.
Great. So let's talk a little bit about the notification process that happens along with the smart contract updates,
given the chain code and the transactions that are happening.
Exactly. So from an IoT perspective, we did set those thresholds in place in order to identify when the IoT sensors would need to notify
a contractor or notify utility company that something doesn't look or feel right.
And that would come up as something as simple as a mobile notification.
We created a mobile app really to make this as simple as possible for our utility companies.
So we had to do a lot more information on it, and we had to do a lot of information on it.
So we had to do a lot of information on the chain code, right?
The other thing that's happening on the chain code, right, is it's moving the workflow along.
So really what we're seeing happening is as different parties do their intended action,
that information is being written to the chain, and we're moving forward in the process.
If the wrong actor tries to do something that they're not entitled to, that's another situation where we
got notifications and the process stops.
Great. So any type of asset agitation along the way that's not supposed to happen, the ledger's being updated, notifications are being sent out.
And I love the fact that you're creating this immutable digital ledger, making sure that everything is being tracked accordingly.
And it's really helping with the transparency, compliance, and regulatory bodies that you discuss, listening closely to your customers in true, again, GSI fashion here.

And at the end of the day, what's very important is that this platform is really combating against the authenticity issues that the supply chain industry is really facing, as well as the counterfeit black market goods that are making their ways around.
Exactly. It's twofold. It's adding this transparency into our supply chain so we can ensure the end-to-end providence of every single thing coming in.
And then it's also a little bit to make the lives of our poor project managers a little bit easy.
Again, reconciling a source of truth between all these different parties is a manual process. It's very reconciliation intensive.
And so when we have that shared ledger just connecting everything on the back end, we're able to relieve some of that stress.
The platform is solving a very big issue out there within the industry, and we really appreciate your time today.
Thanks for having me.
Thanks a lot, Jen. And thank you for joining us on This Is My Architecture.
